skill.name: file-path-traversal-testing
skill.description: Guides authorized security testers through file path traversal assessment, including discovery, payload selection, evidence collection, and remediation advice.
skill.summary: Assess file path traversal exposure in authorized web application security tests.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings for filesystem access, network commands, and dangerous command payloads are largely true positives in context. The content is an offensive path traversal testing guide with explicit instructions to read sensitive files and escalate LFI to command execution, though it also contains remediation and authorized-scope notes. No evidence found of prompt injection or hidden executable malware.

high_findings.title: Actionable Path Traversal Exploitation Guidance
high_findings.description: The skill provides direct traversal payloads and bypass methods for reaching files outside the intended web root, including system password files. This confirms the filesystem and blocker findings as real dual-use risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The payloads are explicit and repeated across exploitation and quick reference sections. They target sensitive operating system files rather than only benign test fixtures.

high_findings.title: Sensitive Credential and System File Target Lists
high_findings.description: The guide enumerates high-value files such as SSH private keys, process environment data, password hashes, and Windows SAM files. This could enable credential theft if used outside authorized scope.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The listed targets include recognized credential stores and private key paths. The semantic context explicitly frames them as high-value files to target.

high_findings.title: LFI to Remote Code Execution Escalation Steps
high_findings.description: The skill includes log poisoning, process environment inclusion, PHP wrapper abuse, and command execution examples. These instructions go beyond detection into exploitation escalation.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The section title and commands directly describe escalation from file inclusion to command execution. The PHP system payloads confirm the static system-call findings as dangerous content.

high_findings.title: Automated Fuzzing Against Web Targets
high_findings.description: The guide provides ffuf and wfuzz commands for testing URL parameters against traversal wordlists. These network commands are legitimate in authorized tests but risky for marketplace publication without strong controls.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are clear external network requests to target web applications. The target host is a placeholder, so the concern is misuse potential rather than exfiltration to a fixed endpoint.

medium_findings.title: External Command Findings Are Mostly Markdown Examples
medium_findings.description: Many static external command matches are inside markdown code blocks and inline examples, not executable skill code. They still provide operational commands that a model could ask a user to run.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is a markdown-only skill and does not contain a runnable Ruby or shell program. However, the commands are actionable guidance for external tools.

medium_findings.title: Authorized Scope Guidance Is Late and Brief
medium_findings.description: The skill includes a short reminder to respect authorized scope, but it appears after extensive exploitation details. It lacks a required consent check or refusal boundary for unauthorized targets.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The authorized-scope note is present, which reduces confidence of malicious intent. Its placement and brevity leave substantial misuse risk for a marketplace skill.

low_findings.title: Ruby Backtick Static Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick execution patterns correspond to markdown code fences and inline code formatting. No standalone Ruby file or runtime command execution implementation is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line context shows markdown examples and tables rather than executable Ruby backticks. The risk comes from the instructional content, not from code that runs automatically.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Placeholder Targets
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL findings use target.com examples for testing commands. No evidence found of a fixed attacker-controlled endpoint or data exfiltration destination.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are generic placeholders used in examples. They remain risky as operational attack templates, but they are not evidence of embedded exfiltration infrastructure.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography static findings do not correspond to cryptographic implementation or configuration. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, or similar weak algorithm use in executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are markdown description or file path examples, not crypto operations. Confidence is slightly lower because the scanner did not provide the exact matched token.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Labels Are Subsumed by File Disclosure Risk
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance findings reference target file examples, command examples, and testing cautions. They are not separate hidden reconnaissance code, but they reinforce the high-risk file disclosure guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible instructional content rather than covert host enumeration logic. They still support the broader conclusion that the skill enables sensitive discovery.

dangerous_patterns.title: Traversal Payloads for Protected Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Multiple sections show traversal sequences intended to access operating system password files and other protected resources.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The same sensitive-file payload pattern appears in basic exploitation, bypass, methodology, and quick reference sections.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential File Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill lists SSH keys, authorized keys, password hashes, process environment files, and Windows SAM files as target files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: These are well-known credential and secret locations. The text presents them as high-value targets.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Execution Payloads Through LFI
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes PHP system payloads and wrapper-based execution techniques for turning file inclusion into command execution.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly include system execution payloads and wrapper schemes. The surrounding heading confirms the intended escalation path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Fuzzing Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes commands for ffuf, wfuzz, and curl against web URLs with traversal payloads.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples are operational network commands. They use placeholders, so they are not a fixed exfiltration route.

content.value_statement: Path traversal flaws can expose server files, credentials, and application code. This skill structures authorized testing and reporting, but it contains high-risk exploit guidance.
actual_capabilities: Explains how path traversal vulnerabilities occur in web applications.
Identifies file-related parameters and application features to inspect.
Lists traversal payload categories, encoding variants, and bypass techniques.
Describes Linux, Windows, web server, and application files that may be exposed.
Provides remediation examples using allowlists and canonical path checks.
Outlines report deliverables for vulnerability evidence and impact.
limitations: Contains offensive payloads that require strict authorization before use.
Does not include an enforced consent check or target validation workflow.
May trigger security tools, logging alerts, or web application firewalls.
Examples use placeholder targets and must not be used on real systems without permission.

use_cases.title: Validate a File Download Endpoint
use_cases.description: Review file parameters, test for directory escape behavior, and document evidence within an approved assessment scope.

use_cases.title: Improve Developer Remediation
use_cases.description: Understand risky path construction patterns and replace them with allowlists, basename handling, and canonical path validation.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Security Training Exercise
use_cases.description: Create a controlled lab that demonstrates file disclosure risks and teaches safer file access patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Review a File Feature
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me review an authorized file download feature for path traversal risk. Focus on safe checks, expected evidence, and remediation notes.

prompt_templates.title: Plan an Authorized Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a path traversal test plan for this approved target. Include scope boundaries, low-impact checks, logging considerations, and reporting steps.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Defensive Code
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this file access design for traversal weaknesses. Recommend safer validation, canonicalization, and allowlist controls.

prompt_templates.title: Write a Finding
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn my authorized test observations into a professional path traversal finding with impact, evidence summary, severity rationale, and remediation.

output_examples.input: I need to assess a file download route in an approved staging environment.
output_examples.output: The skill would produce a scoped test checklist, safe evidence collection steps, expected response indicators, and remediation guidance.

output_examples.input: A developer asks why simple string filtering is not enough.
output_examples.output: The skill would explain canonical paths, allowlists, nested encoding risks, and how to verify the resolved file stays inside the intended directory.

output_examples.input: I have confirmed file disclosure and need a report.
output_examples.output: The skill would draft a finding with affected parameter, impact, proof summary, risk rating, reproduction notes, and practical fixes.

best_practices: Use only in environments where written authorization and scope are clear.
Prefer low-impact validation and avoid collecting sensitive file contents when proof can be minimized.
Pair every confirmed issue with remediation guidance and retest criteria.
anti_patterns: Do not test public or third-party systems without explicit permission.
Do not extract private keys, password hashes, or personal data unless the scope specifically requires it.
Do not treat placeholder commands as safe defaults for production targets.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: No. It contains actionable exploitation and credential-targeting guidance, so it should not be published without stronger safeguards.

faq.question: Does the skill contain executable malware?
faq.answer: No evidence found. The reviewed file is markdown guidance, but the guidance includes high-risk commands and payloads.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high instead of safe?
faq.answer: The skill teaches how to access sensitive files and escalate file inclusion to command execution.

faq.question: Can defenders use this content?
faq.answer: Yes, in authorized labs or assessments. The content should be constrained to defensive and approved testing workflows.

faq.question: What safeguards are missing?
faq.answer: The skill lacks an upfront consent gate, refusal guidance for unauthorized targets, and data minimization rules before exploit steps.

faq.question: What remediation topics does it include?
faq.answer: It covers filename allowlists, basename handling, real path checks, and validating resolved paths against a trusted base directory.
